romania.title: Rezultatele la Evaluarea Națională 2026 au fost publicate. Elevii pot verifica notele online. Situația pe județe, conform Edu.ro
romania.summary: Cu aprobarea Comisiei Naționale de Organizare a Evaluării Naționale (EN VIII) pentru absolvenții clasei a VIII-a, rezultatele inițiale înregistrate în sesiunea din acest an a acestui examen au fost publicate marți, 30 iunie, ora 17:00, potrivit Ministerului Educației.

romania.title: Câți elevi au luat media 10 la Evaluarea Națională 2026. Ministerul Educației a publicat mai devreme rezultatele inițiale
romania.summary: Ministerul Educației a publicat marți, 30 iunie, cu o zi mai devreme decât prevedea calendarul inițial al examenului, rezultatele inițiale ale Evaluării Naționale 2026. Potrivit datelor oficiale, șapte absolvenți de gimnaziu au obținut media 10 înainte de soluționarea contestațiilor.

romania.title: Vești bune privind Autostrada Transilvania: „Ritm susținut” pe un tronson abandonat de Bechtel / Șanse pentru o finalizare înainte de termen
romania.summary: „Se lucrează în ritm susținut pe lotul Chiribiș-Biharia al Autostrăzii A3 Transilvania, șantier ajuns la 60% execuție”, anunță Compania de Investiții Rutiere (CNIR). Tronsonul are termen de finalizare mai 2027, dar sunt șanse bune să fie gata înainte de termen, chiar de la final de 2026. „Constructorul, Asocierea Precon Transilvania-Citadina 98, lucrează constant cu aproximativ …

romania.title: 😂 Tamaș, revoltat de legea care permite berea pe stadion: „Pot să bea doar suporterii, nu și jucătorii!”
romania.summary: Fostul fotbalist, actual drojdier, Gabriel Tamaş acuză o mare nedreptate în noua lege a stadioanelor! Potrivit lui Tamaş, deşi e un lucru bun că berea va fi în sfârşit permisă la meciuri, e „absolut revoltător” că de aceasta se vor putea bucura doar suporterii din galerie, nu şi jucătorii de pe teren. „După ce că […] The post Tamaș, revoltat de legea care permite berea pe stadion: „Pot să bea doar suporterii, nu și jucătorii!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Contestații Evaluare Națională 2026. Când pot fi depuse de către elevii de clasa a VIII-a. Model de cerere contestație Evaluare Națională
romania.summary: Rezultatele la Evaluarea Națională 2026 au fost publicate marți seară, mai devreme cu o zi, de către Ministerul Educației. Elevii de clasa a VIII-a nemulțumiți de notele obținute vor putea depune contestații timp de trei zile, în perioada 1-3 iulie. Înainte de a solicita recorectarea lucrării, candidații au posibilitatea să își vizualizeze tezele. În articol, …

romania.title: 😂 Psihopatul din Pipera! Un corporatist îşi încheie mailurile cu „Salutări călduroase” şi „O zi însorită”
romania.summary: În plină arşiţă, când mii de oameni tânjesc după cea mai mică adiere de răcoare, există monştri cărora suferinţa semenilor le produce o plăcere aproape sexuală! Printre aceştia se numără Dănuţ, un tânăr corporatist din Pipera poreclit de tot etajul „Psihopatul”. Potrivit mai multor colegi, acesta are obiceiul sadic de a-şi încheia mailurile şi mesajele […] The post Psihopatul din Pipera! Un corporatist îşi încheie mailurile cu „Salutări călduroase” şi „O zi însorită” appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Câtă modestie! Patriarhul refuză postul de premier, spunând că îi ajunge Ministerul de Finanţe
romania.summary: Trăim vremuri în care toți aleargă disperați după funcții și bani, dar iată că încă mai avem și câte un exemplu de modestie veritabilă. Porpus de un grup parlamentar pentru funcția de premier, PAtriarhul Daniel ne-a predat o lecție de modestie și a refuzat cu eleganță funcția de șef de Guvern. „Sunt alții mai potriviți […] The post Câtă modestie! Patriarhul refuză postul de premier, spunând că îi ajunge Ministerul de Finanţe appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Newer insulin may reduce low blood sugar in youth with type 1 diabetes
medical.summary: A trial led by University of Pittsburgh researchers and published today in The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology adds nuance to the question of whether older human insulins are as effective as insulin analogs in low-resource settings.

medical.title: Extreme temperatures increase emergency department visits among people with dementia, study finds
medical.summary: Exposure to extreme heat and cold is associated with an increased risk of emergency department (ED) visits among people living with dementia, according to preliminary research presented at the European Academy of Neurology (EAN) Congress 2026. The findings provide new evidence that environmental factors can have measurable effects on the health of people with neurological disorders.

medical.title: African genetic data could change how essential medicines are prescribed
medical.summary: The dosage information for drugs used in HIV treatment, malaria, cancer care, pain management and transplantation is largely based on data from European patients and fails to include vital information about how essential drugs work in diverse African populations. A Wits-led policy brief shows that more than 10% of essential medicines may require pharmacogenomic guidance. Pharmacogenomics is the study of how a person's genetic makeup affects their body's response to medications.

medical.title: Restoring lost senses: One technology for both artificial vision and touch
medical.summary: Patients with untreatable conditions such as sight loss or loss of motor function could be closer to a viable technology for restoring their lost sense within a faster time frame. This is due to the discovery that advanced brain interfacing technology used for both touch and vision prostheses is, in fact, almost the same, despite being developed completely separately for more than 50 years.

medical.title: Growing evidence shows sugar substitutes disrupt gut health and metabolism
medical.summary: Since the first introduction of saccharin, an array of artificial and other non-nutritive (i.e., low-calorie or calorie-free) sweeteners have become ubiquitous in the U.S. food supply. However, a growing body of research suggests that these compounds are not inert in the body and may be disrupting metabolism.

medical.title: Rare aging disorder links 'biological clock' to disease
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a rare genetic condition that causes people to age at a much faster rate, offering fresh insights into the aging process. The study shows for the first time how a "biological clock" present in every cell of the body could contribute to age-related diseases.

medical.title: Mapping the risk factors for mother-infant bonding disorder
medical.summary: Mother-to-infant bonding difficulties (MIBD) are commonly associated with postnatal depression. MIBD has been linked to developmental delays and child abuse. Researchers found that about half of MIBD cases in Japan involve mothers who did not experience postpartum depression. Difficulty holding a fussy baby, lack of happiness about pregnancy and low social support predict an increased risk of MIBD. Clinicians observing these signs should intervene to ensure maternal and child well-being.

medical.title: Breast milk gives certain gut bacteria a head start
medical.summary: Breast milk helps shape the gut microbiota for longer than previously thought. Researchers from DTU and Rigshospitalet have discovered that sugars in breast milk, which are nondigestible by the infant—so-called human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs)—influence which bacteria thrive in the gut during the transition to solid food, and that this influence persists later in life. The findings have been published in Nature Communications.

medical.title: Rising summer heat linked to higher US youth suicide rates, especially ages 15 to 24
medical.summary: From India to the U.S. and across Europe, millions are enduring an intense heat wave as temperatures soar to an unbearable range. Summers over the past few years have been extremely hot in these regions because of the combined effects of climate change and persistent weather patterns that amplify these sweltering conditions.

medical.title: Financial strain may affect health outcomes for people with COPD
medical.summary: The financial cost of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) negatively affects people's mental health and leads them to forgo medications, delay or avoid medical care, and alter major financial plans, according to a new study in the May 2026 issue of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Diseases: Journal of the COPD Foundation.

medical.title: Gene discovery could help prevent stroke in young people
medical.summary: UVA Health stroke researchers have identified a distinct and temporary pattern of gene behavior during cervical artery dissections, a leading cause of stroke in young people. These unusual gene changes may help explain the dissections and could lead to new ways to prevent potentially debilitating strokes among young people.

medical.title: Canada is getting old—and that's our biggest advantage for 2067
medical.summary: Canada will cross the threshold into a "superaged" society this year, with at least 1 in 5 people older than 65.

medical.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
medical.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

medical.title: Scientists discover how a single cell builds a brain with 170 billion cells
medical.summary: How does a single cell build a brain with billions of precisely organized neurons? Researchers suggest that brain cells use their lineage—their cellular family tree—as a kind of positional map. Cells that come from the same ancestor stay near one another, helping the brain organize itself without relying solely on chemical signals.

medical.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
medical.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

science.title: The largest digital camera ever built begins decade-long survey of the universe
science.summary: The largest digital camera ever built is starting to capture images of unseen corners of the universe.

science.title: Houston power plant emerges as dominant source of cloud-forming aerosols
science.summary: Research by atmospheric scientists at UC San Diego's Scripps Institution of Oceanography and colleagues pinpointed an individual coal-fired power plant in Houston as the main source of particles most likely to encourage the formation of clouds around the metropolitan area.

science.title: Physicists demonstrate Hong–Ou–Mandel interference with more than 10 atoms
science.summary: In a new study published in Nature Physics, researchers have demonstrated the Hong–Ou–Mandel (HOM) effect with up to 12 indistinguishable neutral atoms—an effect that has been predominantly observed in photonic systems.

science.title: Adversity can follow NZ kids to the classroom. Can schools make a difference?
science.summary: By their eighth birthday, an estimated 9 in 10 New Zealand children will have experienced some form of serious adversity. They might have been neglected, grown up with family violence, lived through a separation or coped with a parent's mental illness or substance use problem.

science.title: Human activity has not always harmed biodiversity—quite the opposite
science.summary: For millennia, farming in Switzerland did not reduce plant diversity but helped increase it, University of Basel researchers have shown in a detailed reconstruction covering the past 7,000 years. Only recent decades paint a different picture.

science.title: The little red galaxies that may be sending us neutrinos
science.summary: Peering far into the distant, high-redshift universe, the James Webb telescope has discovered an abundance of small red galaxies known as the Little Red Dots. From their observations, astronomers believe that at least some of these galaxies may be home to growing supermassive black holes at their centers, objects which they believe are embedded in dense gaseous envelopes, an environment suitable for producing high-energy neutrinos.

science.title: An iconic spear-throwing device likely wasn't used by prehistoric hunters until around 10,000 years ago
science.summary: Archaeologists have long pictured prehistoric hunters taking down mammoths and other megafauna using the atlatl, a handheld spear-throwing device that uses leverage to achieve greater velocity and force when throwing darts. In particular, use of the atlatl was thought to be common in the Clovis culture in North America, which occurred roughly between 13,340 and 12,710 years ago. However, new research, published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, suggests that the atlatl probably didn't exist in the region until much later.

science.title: Starry Chandelier Cluster
science.summary: This image released on June 26, 2026, features the globular cluster NGC 6723, sometimes called the Chandelier Cluster. Like its namesake, it sparkles with countless lights. However, each ‘lightbulb’ in this chandelier is an individual star 27,000 light-years away in the constellation Sagittarius (the Archer). Globular clusters like NGC 6723 contain some of the oldest […]

science.title: Alive and kicking—walking soccer players defy stereotypes and show fitness is for everyone
science.summary: Older people are defying stereotypes of aging by playing walking soccer, research from Cardiff University shows. Walking soccer is advertised as an accessible sport that provides a safe, sociable and inclusive physical activity. Rules vary depending on the respective club or league, but two well-established stipulations are that players must walk, not run, and excessive physical contact is prohibited.

science.title: New findings on how malaria parasites invade human cells yield proof of concept for new antimalarial drug
science.summary: For nearly half a century, scientists have known that malaria parasites force their way into human red blood cells through a ring-shaped structure called the moving junction. What no one could work out was what it actually does. The structure assembles, does its job and dissipates in the space of 60 seconds—gone before anyone can get a close look.

science.title: School smartphone bans are overly simplistic and not supported by young people, study finds
science.summary: Outright smartphone bans in schools are likely to be ineffective and undermine students' trust without addressing core issues like harmful online content, cyberbullying and addictive platform design, a new UCL report finds.

science.title: Dads want to work from home, but fear career penalties
science.summary: Working from home could improve family well-being, gender equality, fertility and staff retention, but only if fathers can use it without stigma or career penalties, new research from King's College London finds. The researchers analyzed data from the Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes UK (SWAA-UK), Understanding Society and the Labor Force Survey. For return-to-office analysis in SWAA-UK, this included responses from 8,123 full-time working fathers (35+ hours per week) collected in June 2025.

science.title: Camouflaging snails change color in the rain
science.summary: How does a stripy tree snail hide from hungry birds? The Hypselostyla camelopardalis from the Philippines and Reinia variegata from Japan have both evolved a form of dynamic camouflage to survive. Their light-colored patterns vanish in the rain, and the shell turns dark brown, similar to damp tree bark.

science.title: Rice grown on the moon? Air-to-fertilizer technology helps rice grow in lunar soil simulant
science.summary: Securing sustainable food supplies is a key challenge for long-term human exploration and potential habitation of the moon. The moon's soil contains no organic material, and essential plant nitrogen sources like ammonia and nitrate are virtually nonexistent.

science.title: Women hold just 3% of jobs in tourism's biggest transport sector, global study finds
science.summary: Women remain vastly underrepresented in tourism transport jobs worldwide, making up just 3% of land passenger transport workers (such as bus and train staff) in countries with available data, according to a University of Surrey-led report.

science.title: Open cluster NGC 6134 in Norma is 1.38 billion years old and hosts a core, tidal tail and diffuse halo
science.summary: Indonesian astronomers have conducted a comprehensive study of an open cluster in the constellation Norma, known as NGC 6134. Results of the new study, available in a research paper published June 23 on the preprint server arXiv, deliver important insights into the properties and nature of this cluster.

science.title: Linguistic reason Barbie's iconic speech became a cultural moment, and what Aristotle has to do with it
science.summary: Everyone remembers where they were when Gloria lost it. The Barbie movie's big speech—America Ferrera, voice breaking, listing every single impossible thing the world expects women to be—hit something that felt almost too real for a film about a plastic doll.

science.title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
science.summary: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.

science.title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
science.summary: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

However, each ‘lightbulb’ in this chandelier is an individual star 27,000 light-years away in the constellation Sagittarius (the Archer). Globular clusters like NGC 6723 contain some of the oldest […]

mix_items.title: Alive and kicking—walking soccer players defy stereotypes and show fitness is for everyone
mix_items.summary: Older people are defying stereotypes of aging by playing walking soccer, research from Cardiff University shows. Walking soccer is advertised as an accessible sport that provides a safe, sociable and inclusive physical activity. Rules vary depending on the respective club or league, but two well-established stipulations are that players must walk, not run, and excessive physical contact is prohibited.

mix_items.title: Mapping the risk factors for mother-infant bonding disorder
mix_items.summary: Mother-to-infant bonding difficulties (MIBD) are commonly associated with postnatal depression. MIBD has been linked to developmental delays and child abuse. Researchers found that about half of MIBD cases in Japan involve mothers who did not experience postpartum depression. Difficulty holding a fussy baby, lack of happiness about pregnancy and low social support predict an increased risk of MIBD. Clinicians observing these signs should intervene to ensure maternal and child well-being.

mix_items.title: New findings on how malaria parasites invade human cells yield proof of concept for new antimalarial drug
mix_items.summary: For nearly half a century, scientists have known that malaria parasites force their way into human red blood cells through a ring-shaped structure called the moving junction. What no one could work out was what it actually does. The structure assembles, does its job and dissipates in the space of 60 seconds—gone before anyone can get a close look.

mix_items.title: Breast milk gives certain gut bacteria a head start
mix_items.summary: Breast milk helps shape the gut microbiota for longer than previously thought. Researchers from DTU and Rigshospitalet have discovered that sugars in breast milk, which are nondigestible by the infant—so-called human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs)—influence which bacteria thrive in the gut during the transition to solid food, and that this influence persists later in life. The findings have been published in Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Rising summer heat linked to higher US youth suicide rates, especially ages 15 to 24
mix_items.summary: From India to the U.S. and across Europe, millions are enduring an intense heat wave as temperatures soar to an unbearable range. Summers over the past few years have been extremely hot in these regions because of the combined effects of climate change and persistent weather patterns that amplify these sweltering conditions.

mix_items.title: School smartphone bans are overly simplistic and not supported by young people, study finds
mix_items.summary: Outright smartphone bans in schools are likely to be ineffective and undermine students' trust without addressing core issues like harmful online content, cyberbullying and addictive platform design, a new UCL report finds.

mix_items.title: Financial strain may affect health outcomes for people with COPD
mix_items.summary: The financial cost of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) negatively affects people's mental health and leads them to forgo medications, delay or avoid medical care, and alter major financial plans, according to a new study in the May 2026 issue of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Diseases: Journal of the COPD Foundation.

mix_items.title: Dads want to work from home, but fear career penalties
mix_items.summary: Working from home could improve family well-being, gender equality, fertility and staff retention, but only if fathers can use it without stigma or career penalties, new research from King's College London finds. The researchers analyzed data from the Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes UK (SWAA-UK), Understanding Society and the Labor Force Survey. For return-to-office analysis in SWAA-UK, this included responses from 8,123 full-time working fathers (35+ hours per week) collected in June 2025.

mix_items.title: Gene discovery could help prevent stroke in young people
mix_items.summary: UVA Health stroke researchers have identified a distinct and temporary pattern of gene behavior during cervical artery dissections, a leading cause of stroke in young people. These unusual gene changes may help explain the dissections and could lead to new ways to prevent potentially debilitating strokes among young people.

mix_items.title: Camouflaging snails change color in the rain
mix_items.summary: How does a stripy tree snail hide from hungry birds? The Hypselostyla camelopardalis from the Philippines and Reinia variegata from Japan have both evolved a form of dynamic camouflage to survive. Their light-colored patterns vanish in the rain, and the shell turns dark brown, similar to damp tree bark.

mix_items.title: Rice grown on the moon? Air-to-fertilizer technology helps rice grow in lunar soil simulant
mix_items.summary: Securing sustainable food supplies is a key challenge for long-term human exploration and potential habitation of the moon. The moon's soil contains no organic material, and essential plant nitrogen sources like ammonia and nitrate are virtually nonexistent.

mix_items.title: Women hold just 3% of jobs in tourism's biggest transport sector, global study finds
mix_items.summary: Women remain vastly underrepresented in tourism transport jobs worldwide, making up just 3% of land passenger transport workers (such as bus and train staff) in countries with available data, according to a University of Surrey-led report.

mix_items.title: Canada is getting old—and that's our biggest advantage for 2067
mix_items.summary: Canada will cross the threshold into a "superaged" society this year, with at least 1 in 5 people older than 65.

mix_items.title: Open cluster NGC 6134 in Norma is 1.38 billion years old and hosts a core, tidal tail and diffuse halo
mix_items.summary: Indonesian astronomers have conducted a comprehensive study of an open cluster in the constellation Norma, known as NGC 6134. Results of the new study, available in a research paper published June 23 on the preprint server arXiv, deliver important insights into the properties and nature of this cluster.

mix_items.title: Linguistic reason Barbie's iconic speech became a cultural moment, and what Aristotle has to do with it
mix_items.summary: Everyone remembers where they were when Gloria lost it. The Barbie movie's big speech—America Ferrera, voice breaking, listing every single impossible thing the world expects women to be—hit something that felt almost too real for a film about a plastic doll.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
mix_items.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover how a single cell builds a brain with 170 billion cells
mix_items.summary: How does a single cell build a brain with billions of precisely organized neurons? Researchers suggest that brain cells use their lineage—their cellular family tree—as a kind of positional map. Cells that come from the same ancestor stay near one another, helping the brain organize itself without relying solely on chemical signals.

mix_items.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
mix_items.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

mix_items.title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
mix_items.summary: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Azi n-am procrastinat deloc. Am amanat direct toata ziua.

satire_ro.title: Turneu Stand-up Comedy cu Călin Gheorghiu: Caut iubită!
